title: Comparative advantage and absolute advantage
description: Practice what you have learned about comparative advantage and absolute advantage in this exercise.
topic_path: Comparative advantage and the terms of trade

assessment_items.question_content:
Max and Eli like to build apps and coach soccer. The time it takes for Max and Eli to build apps and plan soccer drills for their team is given in the table below.

| Max | Eli
-|-
Time to build an app | $20$ hours | $15$ hours
Time to write a soccer drill | $5$ hours | $9$ hours




**Which person has an absolute advantage in building apps, and which person has an absolute advantage in planning soccer drills?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Eli has an absolute advantage in building apps; Max has an absolute advantage in planning soccer drills.
Eli has an absolute advantage in producing both goods.
Neither person has an absolute advantage in producing either good.
Both have an absolute advantage in producing both goods.
Max has an absolute advantage in building apps; nobody has an absolute advantage in planning soccer drills.

assessment_items.question_content:
Alex and Ella are completing a project for class together that requires them to complete worksheets and write essays, and they are trying to decide how to best use their time to divide and conquer this assignment. 

The input table below gives the time that it takes Ella and Alex to complete their homework assignments in hours.

 | Time to complete Worksheets | Time to complete Essays
- | - |
Ella | $1$ hour | $2$ hours
Alex  | $1$ hour | $3$ hours 

**Who has absolute advantage in each good?**
[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: Alex has absolute advantage in essays and Ella has absolute advantage in worksheets
Ella has absolute advantage in essays and Alex has absolute advantage in worksheets
Both have absolute advantage in both goods.
Ella has absolute advantage in essays, but neither has absolute advantage in worksheets
Alex has absolute advantage in essays, but neither has absolute advantage in worksheets

assessment_items.question_content:
Martha and Dave like to make identical tacos and cakes. The time it takes Martha and Dave to make tacos and cakes is given in the table below.

 | Martha | Dave
-|-
Time to make a taco | $10$ minutes | $15$ minutes
Time to make a cake | $20$ minutes | $30$ minutes

**Which person has an absolute advantage in the production of tacos, and which person has an absolute advantage in the production of cakes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Martha has an absolute advantage in producing both goods.
Dave has an absolute advantage in producing both goods.
Neither Dave nor Martha has an absolute advantage at producing either good.
Both Martha and Dave have an absolute advantage in producing both goods.
Martha has an absolute advantage in tacos; Dave has an absolute advantage in cakes.

assessment_items.question_content:
Two agents with identical resources can each make the same two goods:  baseball trophies and racing medals.

**Which of the following best captures the idea of absolute advantage for these two agents?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: An agent is able to produce more of a good or service than another economic agent given the same resources.
One economic agent has a lower opportunity cost of one good, and another economic agent has a lower opportunity cost of a different good.
Both agents have identical opportunity costs.
One agent has higher opportunity costs of one good, and the other agent has higher opportunity costs of the other good.
One agent is able to sell its goods at for a higher price than the other.

assessment_items.question_content:
Kim and Leah have the same amount of time and resources available. Kim can write either $10$ articles or complete $5$ worksheets. Leah can either write $3$ articles or complete $9$ worksheets.

**Who has an absolute advantage in each thing?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: An agent has an absolute advantage in production if that agent can produce more of a good than another agent, given the same resources.
Given the same resources, Kim can write 10 articles and Leah can write 3 articles.
Kim can produce more articles than Leah given the same resources. Therefore Kim has absolute advantage in writing articles.
Given the same resources, Kim can write complete 5 worksheets and Leah can complete 9 worksheets.
Leah can produce more worksheets than Kim, and therefore Leah has absolute advantage in producing worksheets.

choices.content: Kim has an absolute advantage in writing articles; Leah has an absolute advantage in worksheets.
Kim has an absolute advantage in worksheets; Leah has an absolute advantage in writing articles.
Kim has an absolute advantage in both goods.
Leah has an absolute advantage in both goods.
Nobody has an absolute advantage in either good.

assessment_items.question_content:
Tomer and Charlotte have the same resources and time. Tomer can either write $100$ lines of code or process $20$ reports, and Charlotte can either write $120$ lines of code or process $20$ reports.

**Who has an absolute advantage in each thing?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: An agent has an absolute advantage in production if that agent can produce more of a good than another agent, given the same resources.
Given the same resources, Tomer can write $100$ lines of code and Charlotte can write $120$ lines of code.
Charlotte has absolute advantage in writing code because she can produce more lines of code than Tomer.
Given the same resources, Tomer can process $20$ reports, and Charlotte process $20$ reports.
Nobody has absolute advantage in processing reports because nobody produces more reports than anyone else.

choices.content: Charlotte has an absolute advantage in writing lines of code; nobody has an absolute advantage in processing reports.
Charlotte has an absolute advantage in writing code; both people have an absolute advantage in processing reports.
Charlotte has an absolute advantage in both goods.
Tomer has an absolute advantage in both goods.
Charlotte has an absolute advantage in writing code and Tomer has an absolute advantage in processing reports.

assessment_items.question_content:
Aanand can either build $6$ models or prepare $4$ experiments, and Pam can either build $9$ models or prepare $6$ experiments. Aanand and Pam have identical time and resources available.

**Who has an absolute advantage in building models and who has an absolute advantage in preparing experiments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: An agent has an absolute advantage in production if that agent can produce more of a good than another agent, given the same resources.
Given the same resources, Aanand can  build $6$ models and Pam can build $9$ models.
Pam has an absolute advantage in building models because she can build more models than Aanand.
Given the same resources, Aanand can prepare $4$ experiments and Pam can prepare $6$ experiments.
Pam also has an absolute advantage in preparing experiments because she can produce more experiments than Aanand, given the same resources.

choices.content: Pam has an absolute advantage in both building models and preparing experiments.
Pam has an absolute advantage in building models; both people have an absolute advantage in preparing experiments.
Pam has an absolute advantage in building models and Aanand has an absolute advantage at preparing experiments.
Aanand has an absolute advantage in both goods.
Aanand has an absolute advantage in building models and Pam has an absolute advantage in preparing experiments.

assessment_items.question_content:
Eric and Melanie like to build apps and coach soccer. The time it takes Eric and Melanie to build apps and plan soccer drills for their team is given in the table below.

| Eric | Melanie
-|-
Time to build an app | $20$ hours | $20$ hours
Time to write a soccer drill | $5$ hours | $10$ hours

**Which person has a comparative advantage in the production of apps, and which person has a comparative advantage in the production of soccer drills?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Melanie has a comparative advantage in building apps; Eric has a comparative advantage in creating soccer drills.
Melanie has a comparative advantage in creating soccer drills; Eric has a comparative advantage in building apps.
Neither person has a comparative advantage at producing either good.
Both people have a comparative advantage in producing both goods.
Eric has a comparative advantage in building apps; nobody has a comparative advantage in planning soccer drills.

assessment_items.question_content:
The PPC below shows how many airplanes and pizzas Country A and Country B can produce with a certain number of workers.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which country has comparative advantage in pizza?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The country that has comparative advantage in pizza is the country that gives up fewer airplanes. We can figure this out by comparing the slopes of the PPCs.
Country A gives up one airplane for each one million pizzas, and Country B gives up 1.5 airplanes per one million pizzas. Therefore, Country A has comparative advantage in producing pizzas.

options.alt: A graph with two axes depicting a trade-off between two goods: airplanes and pizzas. The vertical axis represents the number of airplanes, which ranges from 0 to 8. The horizontal axis represents the number of pizzas, measured in millions, and extends from 0 to 8 as well. Two lines, labeled A and B, illustrate different production possibilities or trade-offs between the two goods. Line A has a negative slope from (0, 8) and (8, 0). Line B has a negative slope from (0, 6) and (4, 0).
options.caption: The production possibilities of two countries

choices.content: Country A
Country B
Both Country A and Country B
Neither Country A nor Country B
We cannot determine this

assessment_items.question_content:
Martha and Dave like to make tacos and cakes. The time it takes for Martha and Dave to make tacos and cakes are given in the table below.

| Martha | Dave
-|-
Time to make a taco | $10$ minutes | $15$ minutes
Time to make a cake | $20$ minutes | $30$ minutes


**Which person has a comparative advantage in the production of tacos, and which person has a comparative advantage in the production of cakes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Neither person has comparative advantage in producing either good.
Martha has a comparative advantage in producing both goods.
Dave has a comparative advantage in producing both goods.
Both have a comparative advantage in producing both goods.
They both have a comparative advantage in producing tacos; neither person has a comparative advantage in producing cakes.

assessment_items.question_content:
Alex and Henry are completing group work for class. The input table below gives the time that it takes Henry and Alex to complete their homework assignments in hours.

 | Time to complete Worksheets | Time to complete Essays
- | - |
Henry | $1$ hour | $2$ hours
Alex  | $1$ hour | $3$ hours 

**Who has comparative advantage in producing essays?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Alex
Henry
Neither Henry nor Alex
Both Henry and Alex
We cannot determine who has comparative advantage

assessment_items.question_content:
Jessica can produce $2$ boats in a day or $100$ umbrellas in a day. Paul can produce $3$ boats in a day or $120$ umbrellas in a day.

This means that for every boat that Jessica produces, her opportunity cost is $50$ umbrellas. For every boat Paul produces, his opportunity cost is $40$ umbrellas.

**Which of the following best illustrates the idea of comparative advantage?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Jessica produces fewer boats in a day than Paul, so she has comparative advantage in producing boats.
Paul is relatively better at producing boats because he gives up fewer umbrellas per boat.
Paul is better at producing boats because he can produce more boats in a day.
There is no comparative advantage in this case because Paul can produce more goods in a day.
Jessica produces fewer umbrellas in a day than Paul, so she has comparative advantage in producing boats.

assessment_items.question_content:
Using the same amount of time and resources, Kim can write either $10$ articles or complete $5$ worksheets and Leah can either write $3$ articles or complete $9$ worksheets.

**Who has a comparative advantage in each thing?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To determine who has a comparative advantage in each good, you must first find each person’s opportunity cost of producing each good and then compare the opportunity costs. Whoever has the lower opportunity cost of a good has a comparative advantage in producing that good.

You can express each person’s opportunity cost as an equation:

$\text{(Maximum number of articles)} \cdot A = \text{(Maximum number of worksheets)} \cdot W$

To find the opportunity cost of articles solve for A, and to find the opportunity cost of worksheets solve for W.

The equation that represents Kim's production possibilities is:

$10 A = 5  W$

We can solve for A to get the opportunity cost of articles:

$\begin{align} 10 A&=5 W ~~~~~~\text{divide each side by } \blue{10} \text{ to get } A \text{ by itself }\\\\
\dfrac{10A}{\blue{10}}&=\dfrac{5W}{\blue{10}}\\\\
\dfrac{\cancel{10}A}{\blue{\cancel{10}}}&=\dfrac{5W}{\blue{10}}\\\\
A&=\dfrac{5W}{\blue{10}}\\\\
A&=\dfrac{5}{10}W~~~~~~ \text{simplify the fraction}\\\\
A&=\dfrac{1}{2}W\end{align}$

Kim’s opportunity cost of an article is $\dfrac{1}{2}$of a worksheet:

$A=\dfrac{1}{2}W$

To find Kim's opportunity cost of a worksheet, solve for W:

$\begin{align}A&=\dfrac{1}{2}W~~~~~~\text{multiply both sides by }\blue{2}\text{ to get W by itself}\\\\
\blue{2}\cdot A &= \dfrac{1}{2} W \cdot \blue{2}\\\\
\blue{2}\cdot A &= \dfrac{1}{\cancel{2}} W \cdot \blue{\cancel{2}}\\\\
\blue{2}\cdot A &=  W \end{align}$

Kim's opportunity cost of a worksheet is $2$ articles:

$2A=W$

Leah's production possibilities are represented by this equation:

$3 A = 9 W$

To find her opportunity cost of an article, solve for $A$:

$\begin{align} 3A&=9 W ~~~~~~\text{divide each side by } \blue{3} \text{ to get } A \text{ by itself }\\\\
\dfrac{3A}{\blue{3}}&=\dfrac{9W}{\blue{3}}\\\\
\dfrac{\cancel{3}A}{\blue{\cancel{3}}}&=\dfrac{9W}{\blue{3}}\\\\
A&=\dfrac{9W}{\blue{3}}\\\\
A&=\dfrac{9}{3}W~~~~~~\text{simplify the fraction}\\\\
A&=3W\end{align}$

Leah's opportunity cost of an article is $3$ worksheets:

$A=3W$

To find Leah's opportunity cost of a worksheet, solve for W:

$\begin{align}A&=3W~~~~~~\text{divide both sides by }\blue{3}\text{ to get W by itself}\\\\
\dfrac{A}{\blue{3}} &= \dfrac{3W}{\blue{3}}\\\\
\dfrac{A}{\blue{3}} &= \dfrac{\cancel{3}W}{\cancel{\blue{3}}} \\\\
\dfrac{A}{\blue{3}} &=  W\\\\
\dfrac{1}{\blue{3}}A &=  W
 \end{align}$

Leah's opportunity cost of a worksheet is $\dfrac{1}{3}$ of an article:

$\dfrac{1}{3} A=W$

To find who has a comparative advantage in producing articles, compare each agent's opportunity cost of producing articles.

Kim’s opportunity cost of an article is ½ of a worksheet and Leah’s opportunity cost of an article is 3 worksheets. 

Therefore, Kim has a comparative advantage in articles.

To find who has a comparative advantage in producing worksheets, compare each agent's opportunity cost of producing worksheets.

Kim’s opportunity cost of a worksheet is $2$ articles and Leah’s opportunity cost of a worksheet is $\dfrac{1}{3}$ of an article. 

Therefore, Leah has a comparative advantage in worksheets.

The answer:

> Kim has a comparative advantage at producing articles, and Leah has a comparative advantage in producing worksheets.

choices.content: Kim has a comparative advantage in writing articles, Leah has a comparative advantage in worksheets.
Kim has a comparative advantage in worksheets; Leah has a comparative advantage in writing articles.
Kim has a comparative advantage in both goods.
Leah has a comparative advantage in both goods.
Nobody has a comparative advantage in either good.

assessment_items.question_content:
Using the same amount of time and resources, Tomer can either write $100$ lines of code or process $20$ reports, and Charlotte can either write $120$ lines of code or process $20$ reports.

**Who has a comparative advantage in each thing?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To determine who has a comparative advantage in each good, you must first find each person’s opportunity cost of producing each good and then compare the opportunity costs. Whoever has the lower opportunity cost of a good has a comparative advantage in producing that good.

You can express each person’s opportunity cost as an equation:

$\text{(Maximum number of lines of code)} \cdot C = \text{(Maximum number of reports)} \cdot  R$

To find the opportunity cost of lines of code solve for C, and to find the opportunity cost of reports solve for R.

The equation that represents Charlotte's production possibilities is:

$120 C = 20 R$

We can solve for C to get the opportunity cost of lines of code:

$\begin{align} 120 C&=20 R ~~~~~~\text{divide each side by } \blue{120} \text{ to get } C \text{ by itself }\\\\
\dfrac{120C}{\blue{120}}&=\dfrac{20R}{\blue{120}}\\\\
\dfrac{\cancel{120}C}{\blue{\cancel{120}}}&=\dfrac{20R}{\blue{120}}\\\\
C&=\dfrac{20R}{\blue{120}}~~~~~~\text{simplfy the fraction}\\\\
C&=\dfrac{1}{6}R\end{align}$

Charlotte's opportunity cost of a line of code is $\dfrac{1}{6}$ of a report:

$C=\dfrac{1}{6}R$

To find Charlotte's opportunity cost of a report, solve for R:

$\begin{align}C&=\dfrac{1}{6}R~~~~~~\text{multiply both sides by }\blue{6}\text{ to get R by itself}\\\\
\blue{6}\cdot C &= \dfrac{1}{6} R \cdot \blue{6}\\\\
\blue{6}\cdot C &= \dfrac{1}{\cancel{6}} R \cdot \blue{\cancel{6}}\\\\
\blue{6}\cdot C &=  R \end{align}$

Charlotte's opportunity cost of a report is $6$ lines of code:

$6C=R$

Tomer's production possibilities are represented by this equation:

$100C = 20 R$


To find their opportunity cost of a line of code, solve for $C$:

$\begin{align} 100C&=20 R ~~~~~~\text{divide each side by } \blue{100} \text{ to get } C \text{ by itself }\\\\
\dfrac{100C}{\blue{100}}&=\dfrac{20R}{\blue{100}}\\\\
\dfrac{\cancel{100}C}{\blue{\cancel{100}}}&=\dfrac{20R}{\blue{100}}\\\\
C&=\dfrac{20R}{\blue{100}}\\\\
C&=\dfrac{20}{100}R~~~~~~\text{simplify the fraction}\\\\
C&=\dfrac{1}{5}R\end{align}$

Tomer's opportunity cost of a line of code is $\dfrac{1}{5}$ 
 of a report:

$C=\dfrac{1}{5}R$

To find Tomer's opportunity cost of a report, solve for R:

$\begin{align}C&=\dfrac{1}{5}R~~~~~~\text{multiply both sides by }\blue{5}\text{ to get R by itself}\\\\
\blue{5}C &= \dfrac{1}{5} R \cdot \blue{5}\\\\
\blue{5}C &= \dfrac{1}{\cancel{5}} R \cdot \blue{\cancel{5}}\\\\
\blue{5}C&=R \end{align}$

Tomer's opportunity cost of a report is $5$ lines of code:

$5C=R$

To find who has a comparative advantage in producing lines of code, compare each agent's opportunity cost of producing lines of code.

Charlotte’s opportunity cost of a line of code is $\dfrac{1}{6}$ of a report and Tomer’s opportunity cost of a line of code is $\dfrac{1}{5}$ of a report. 

Therefore, Charlotte has a comparative advantage in lines of code.

To find who has a comparative advantage in producing reports, compare each agent's opportunity cost of producing reports.

Charlotte’s opportunity cost of a report is 6 lines of codes and Tomer’s opportunity cost of a report is 5 of a line of code. 

Therefore Tomer has a comparative advantage in reports.

The answer:

> Charlotte has a comparative advantage at writing lines of code articles, and Tomer has a comparative advantage in producing reports.

choices.content: Charlotte has a comparative advantage in writing lines of code; Tomer has a comparative advantage in processing reports.
Charlotte has a comparative advantage in writing code; neither person has a comparative advantage in processing reports.
Charlotte has a comparative advantage in both goods.
Tomer has a comparative advantage in both goods.
Neither person has a comparative advantage in either good.

assessment_items.question_content:
Using the same amount of time and resources, Aanand can either build 6 models or prepare 4 experiments, and Pam can either build 9 models or prepare 6 experiments.

**Who has a comparative advantage in building models and who has a comparative advantage in preparing experiments?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints: To determine who has a comparative advantage in each good, you must first find each person’s opportunity cost of producing each good and then compare the opportunity costs. Whoever has the lower opportunity cost of a good has a comparative advantage in producing that good.

You can express each person’s opportunity cost as an equation:

$\text{(Maximum number of models)} \cdot M = \text{(Maximum number of experiments)} \cdot E$

To find the opportunity cost of models solve for M, and to find the opportunity cost of experiments solve for E.

The equation that represents Aanand's production possibilities is:

$6M = 4 E$

We can solve for $M$ to get the opportunity cost of models:

$\begin{align} 6M&=4E ~~~~~~\text{divide each side by } \blue{6} \text{ to get } M \text{ by itself }\\\\
\dfrac{6M}{\blue{6}}&=\dfrac{4E}{\blue{6}}\\\\
\dfrac{\cancel{6}M}{\blue{\cancel{6}}}&=\dfrac{4E}{\blue{6}}\\\\
M&=\dfrac{4}{\blue{6}}E~~~~~~\text{simplify the fraction}\\\\
M&=\dfrac{2}{3}E\end{align}$

Aanand's opportunity cost of a model is $\dfrac{2}{3}$ of an experiment:

$M=\dfrac{2}{3}E$

To find Aanand's opportunity cost of an experiment, solve for E:

$\begin{align}M&=\dfrac{2}{3}E~~~~~~\text{multiply both sides by }\blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}\text{ to get E by itself}\\\\
\blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}\cdot M &= \dfrac{2}{3} E \cdot \blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}~~~~~\text{rearrange the right hand side of the equation to put fractions together}\\\\
\blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}\cdot M &= \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}E \\\\\
\blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}\cdot M &= \dfrac{\cancel{2}}{\cancel{3}} E \cdot \blue{\dfrac{\cancel{3}}{\cancel{2}}}\\\\
\blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}\cdot M &=  E~~~~~\text{simplify the fraction}\\\\
\blue{1.5}M&=E \end{align}$

Aanand's opportunity cost of an experiment is $1.5$ models:

$1.5M=E$

Pam's production possibilities are represented by this equation:

$9M=6E$


To find her opportunity cost of a model, solve for $M$:

$\begin{align} 9M&=6E ~~~~~~\text{divide each side by } \blue{9} \text{ to get } M \text{ by itself }\\\\
\dfrac{9A}{\blue{9}}&=\dfrac{6E}{\blue{9}}\\\\
\dfrac{\cancel{9}M}{\blue{\cancel{9}}}&=\dfrac{6E}{\blue{9}}\\\\
M&=\dfrac{6E}{\blue{9}}\\\\
M&=\dfrac{6}{9}E~~~~~~\text{simplify the fraction}\\\\
M&=\dfrac{2}{3}E\end{align}$

Pam's opportunity cost of a model is $\dfrac{2}{3}$ of an experiment:

$A=\dfrac{2}{3}E$

To find Pam's opportunity cost of an experiment, solve for E:

$\begin{align}M&=\dfrac{2}{3}E~~~~~~\text{multiply both sides by }\blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}\text{ to get E by itself}\\\\
\blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}\cdot M &= \dfrac{2}{3} E \cdot \blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}~~~~~\text{rearrange the right hand side of the equation to put fractions together}\\\\
\blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}\cdot M &= \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}E \\\\\
\blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}\cdot M &= \dfrac{\cancel{2}}{\cancel{3}} E \cdot \blue{\dfrac{\cancel{3}}{\cancel{2}}}\\\\
\blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}\cdot M &=  E~~~~~\text{simplify the fraction}\\\\
\blue{1.5}M&=E \end{align}$

Pam's opportunity cost of an experiment is $\dfrac{2}{3}$ of a model:

$\dfrac{2}{3} A=E$

To find who has a comparative advantage in producing models, compare each agent's opportunity cost of producing models.
Aanand's opportunity cost of a model is $\dfrac{2}{3}$ of an experiment and Pam's opportunity cost of a model is$\dfrac{2}{3}$ experiment. 

Therefore, neither has a comparative advantage in models because they have identical opportunity costs.

To find who has a comparative advantage in preparing experiments, compare each agent's opportunity cost of preparing experiments.
Aanand's opportunity cost of an experiment is $1.5$ models of and Pam's opportunity cost of an experiment is $1.5$ models.

Therefore, neither has a comparative advantage in experiments because they have identical opportunity costs.

The answer:

> Nobody has comparative advantage in either good.

choices.content: Neither has a comparative advantage in producing either good.
Pam has a comparative advantage in both building models and preparing experiments.
Both have a comparative advantage in both goods.
Pam has a comparative advantage in building models; both people have a comparative advantage in preparing experiments.
Aanand has a comparative advantage in building models and Pam has a comparative advantage in preparing experiments.
